title: Jesus love for his father part 2
scripture_text: ‘’After Jesus said this, he looked toward heaven and prayed: “Father, the hour has come. Glorify your Son, that your Son may glorify you. For you granted him authority over all people that he might give eternal life to all those you have given him. Now this is eternal life: that they know you, the only true God, and Jesus Christ, whom you have sent. I have brought you glory on earth by finishing the work you gave me to do. And now, Father, glorify me in your presence with the glory I had with you before the world began.’’
devotional:
1. Jesus lived a life that was submitted to the timing of God.

‘Father the hour has come.’ Ten times in John’s gospel Jesus talks about his hour or time of his suffering. Five times he referred to His hour or His time as not having arrived (John 2:4; 7:6, 8, 30; 8:20). But when the cross was immediately ahead, Jesus said that the hour had come (John 12:23, 27; 13:1; 16:32,17:1)

The word Jesus uses for hour is the Greek word ‘hora,’ which came from Horae who were the mythological gods of the seasons. So as a Son, Jesus related to his father by accepting and submitting to the seasons that God had ordained for his life.

Father the hour has come.

Sometimes in life we have difficult seasons, we have ‘hours’ that are painful and difficult, we have times that are confusing, where we think this could never be of God. We have times where we feel afraid, anxious and out of control. Like Jesus we need to say, ‘Father the hour has come,’ I am terrified, but the hour has come. I am anxious, but the hour has come. In raw vulnerability and in total submission, Jesus embraces this ordained season of darkness. I love that Jesus viewed his passion event as an ‘hour,’ or a season, he knew that it was not going to last for ever, in viewing it has an ‘hour,’ he was expressing confidence in his Father.

2. Jesus wanted his suffering to glorify his Father.

In verse 1, we read, ‘‘After Jesus said this, he looked toward heaven and prayed: “Father, the hour has come. Glorify your Son, that your Son may glorify you.’ Look at verses 4-5 ‘I have brought you glory on earth by finishing the work you gave me to do. And now, Father, glorify me in your presence with the glory I had with you before the world began.’’

To our ears this sounds a very strange, Jesus is asking his father to glorify Him. What is Jesus saying?

Jesus teaches us that a big factor in our relationship with the father is how we handle the inevitable sufferings of life.

In John’s gospel often Jesus’ death, burial and resurrection are referred to as his glory. We need to try and understand what Jesus is asking for.

The cross was an unlikely place to find glory. In that day, they didn’t make jewellery to display the cross attractively on a necklace. That would be like wearing a hangman’s noose around your neck in our day!

The cross was a place of shame, humiliation, and the most excruciating form of execution known to man. So how could the cross be a place of glory?

When we think of glory our minds usually think in terms of beauty, splendour, brightness and at times glory is displayed that way. However, it literally means, “To be heavy or weighty” and has to do with “reputation, fame and prestige.”

So when Jesus was asking his Father to glorify him he was saying something like, ‘Father I am asking that through my arrest, trial, crucifixion, resurrection, ascension and coronation that people will see my true identify. In other words I am asking that despite the depth of the suffering I am going to face, may I suffer with integrity so that who I am will bring glory to you.

When Jesus prayed ‘‘Glorify your son’’, this was a prayer of resolution and commitment. It was an expression of a deep seated desire to glorify his father irrespective of the circumstances. Sometimes God comes and rescues us from difficult circumstances, but most of the time he asks us to go through them so his glory can be seen through us. And this is what happened at the cross.
prayer: Father help me to recognise that sometimes to bring you glory can be a painful thing.
html:
Jesus love for his father part 2

Study Text: John 17 v 1-5
‘’After Jesus said this, he looked toward heaven and prayed: “Father, the hour has come. Glorify your Son, that your Son may glorify you. For you granted him authority over all people that he might give eternal life to all those you have given him. Now this is eternal life: that they know you, the only true God, and Jesus Christ, whom you have sent. I have brought you glory on earth by finishing the work you gave me to do. And now, Father, glorify me in your presence with the glory I had with you before the world began.’’

1. Jesus lived a life that was submitted to the timing of God.

‘Father the hour has come.’ Ten times in John’s gospel Jesus talks about his hour or time of his suffering. Five times he referred to His hour or His time as not having arrived (John 2:4; 7:6, 8, 30; 8:20). But when the cross was immediately ahead, Jesus said that the hour had come (John 12:23, 27; 13:1; 16:32,17:1)

The word Jesus uses for hour is the Greek word ‘hora,’ which came from Horae who were the mythological gods of the seasons. So as a Son, Jesus related to his father by accepting and submitting to the seasons that God had ordained for his life.

Father the hour has come.

Sometimes in life we have difficult seasons, we have ‘hours’ that are painful and difficult, we have times that are confusing, where we think this could never be of God. We have times where we feel afraid, anxious and out of control. Like Jesus we need to say, ‘Father the hour has come,’ I am terrified, but the hour has come. I am anxious, but the hour has come. In raw vulnerability and in total submission, Jesus embraces this ordained season of darkness. I love that Jesus viewed his passion event as an ‘hour,’ or a season, he knew that it was not going to last for ever, in viewing it has an ‘hour,’ he was expressing confidence in his Father.

2. Jesus wanted his suffering to glorify his Father.

In verse 1, we read, ‘‘After Jesus said this, he looked toward heaven and prayed: “Father, the hour has come. Glorify your Son, that your Son may glorify you.’ Look at verses 4-5 ‘I have brought you glory on earth by finishing the work you gave me to do. And now, Father, glorify me in your presence with the glory I had with you before the world began.’’

To our ears this sounds a very strange, Jesus is asking his father to glorify Him. What is Jesus saying?

Jesus teaches us that a big factor in our relationship with the father is how we handle the inevitable sufferings of life.

In John’s gospel often Jesus’ death, burial and resurrection are referred to as his glory. We need to try and understand what Jesus is asking for.

The cross was an unlikely place to find glory. In that day, they didn’t make jewellery to display the cross attractively on a necklace. That would be like wearing a hangman’s noose around your neck in our day!

The cross was a place of shame, humiliation, and the most excruciating form of execution known to man. So how could the cross be a place of glory?

When we think of glory our minds usually think in terms of beauty, splendour, brightness and at times glory is displayed that way. However, it literally means, “To be heavy or weighty” and has to do with “reputation, fame and prestige.”

So when Jesus was asking his Father to glorify him he was saying something like, ‘Father I am asking that through my arrest, trial, crucifixion, resurrection, ascension and coronation that people will see my true identify. In other words I am asking that despite the depth of the suffering I am going to face, may I suffer with integrity so that who I am will bring glory to you.

When Jesus prayed ‘‘Glorify your son’’, this was a prayer of resolution and commitment. It was an expression of a deep seated desire to glorify his father irrespective of the circumstances. Sometimes God comes and rescues us from difficult circumstances, but most of the time he asks us to go through them so his glory can be seen through us. And this is what happened at the cross.

Prayer: Father help me to recognise that sometimes to bring you glory can be a painful thing.
Bible in a year reading plan: - 2 Kings 10-13
